data.title: October Dogs Of The Dow Flash One Ideal 'Safer' Dividend Buy
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Dow Industrials is too pricey and reveals only skinny dividends, one of the ten lowest priced Dogs of October (again) finds only Verizon (VZ), living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price and it is also 'safer' because it has more-than-enough free-cash-flow to support its dividends.

In late February, 2024 Dow Jones replaced the low priced high yield dividend payer WBA, with AMZN, a high-priced non-dividend payer. More recently, in November, 2024, INTC was replaced by NVDA and DOW was supplanted by SHW.

With renewed downside market pressure up to 84% all ten of the top 'safer' October Dow dividend dogs could become elite fair-priced buys with annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced Dow Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the mid-point of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 13.31% To 41.8% Net Gains From Top-Ten Dow Dogs By October 2026

Three of ten top dividend-yielding Dow dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this October, 2026 yield-based forecast for Dow dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 30% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1,000.00 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding Dow stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to October, 2026 were:

Salesforce (CRM) was projected to net $417.99, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 55 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 19% over the market as a whole.

Honeywell (HON) was projected to net $216.85 based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% over the market as a whole.

Amazon (AMZN) was projected to net $195.64, based on the median of target price estimates from 67 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 28% more than the market as a whole.

Coca-Cola (KO) was projected to net $190.06 based on the median of target prices estimated by 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 57% less than the market as a whole.

Microsoft (MSFT) was projected to net $185.17, based on the median of target price estimates from 58 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% greater than the market as a whole.

Disney (DIS) was projected to net $178.14, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 31 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% above the market as a whole.

Boeing (BA) was projected to net $168.44, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 47% greater than the market as a whole.

Verizon was projected to net $161.80, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 37% over the market as a whole.

Merck (MRK) was projected to net $153.21, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 28 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 63% less than the market as a whole.

NVIDIA (NVDA) was projected to net $133.13, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 63 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 112% over the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 20.00% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Dow Index stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 9% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

30 Dow Dogs By 1Yr Total Returns

30 Dow Dogs By Analyst Target Gain Estimates

The October, 2025 Dow 30 By Yield

No October, 2025 Dow 30 Dogs Made the Top Ten in All Three Ranks: Returns, Targets, & Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top October Dow Dividend Stocks By Yield Ranged 2.36% To 6.30%

Top ten Dow Dogs represented six of eleven Morningstar sectors.

The lone communication services dog placed first, Verizon [1], to lead the pack. Then, in second place was another loner; this one from the energy sector, Chevron (CVX) [2].

Four healthcare stocks placed third, fourth, sixth, and eighth: Merck [3], Amgen (AMGN) [4], then Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) [6], and United Health (UNH) [8].

The two October consumer staple stalwarts, placed fifth and seventh, Coca Cola Co [5], and Procter& Gamble (PG) [7].

One technology member placed ninth, Cisco Systems (CSCO) [9]. Finally, the lone consumer discretionary member placed tenth, McDonald's (MCD) [10], to complete the October top-ten Dogs of the Dow by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graphic above shows the relative strengths of the top ten October Dow dogs by dividend and price as of market close 10/1/2025.

Nearly all top-ten Dow dogs show a less than ideal status because the prices of nine exceed the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by just one of the five lowest priced Dogs of the Dow. Only Verizon, lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

Dow Index Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 10/1/25

The above chart reveals thirty Dow dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

Two stocks in the Dow index pay no dividend and are, therefore, excluded from the calculation. For those that count, October's least expensive dog of the Dow is Verizon at $15.88 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is NVIDIA (NVDA) at $4,672.90 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Two of Twenty-Eight Dow Dividend Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividend, The September pair are Coca-Cola, and 3M. They are considered unsafe to buy due to dividend yields exceeding free cash flow yields.

Two cut their dividends in the 2020 Ides of March depression times, Boeing, and The Walt Disney Co. One more, Salesforce, snuck onto the Dow index without a dividend.

However, in March 2023, Salesforce declared its first-ever quarterly payout and Disney, in January, 2024 rejoined the dividend paying ranks. However six (four short on cash and two non-dividend payers, Amazon, and Boeing) cast a pall on the Dow by being stockholder repulsive.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, TACO deals, wars, ICE, and tariffs, too.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Rerates Could Get All Ten Dow Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, one in the top ten is already ideally priced. Beside Verizon, already in the ideal zone, nine more top-yield stocks are within $216.40 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or split shares. Those options may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 12.21% To 42.09% Top Ten Dow Index Upsides To October, 2026; (32),Five -1.81 to -6.33% Downsiders Followed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 36.69% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Dow Dogs To October, 2026

Ten-top Dow October-updated yield (dividend / price) results, as verified by YCharts, made the following ranking:

As noted-above, top-ten Dow dogs selected 10/1/25 by yield, represented six of eleven sectors. (Real Estate is not reported by the Dow and Utilities has its own Dow Index. Materials, Financials, and Industrials also missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield Dow Dogs (34) To Deliver 15.09% Vs. (35) 11.04% Net Gains by All Ten Come October, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dow Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 36.69% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The second lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Coca-Cola, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 19.01%.

The five lowest-priced Dow top-yield dogs for October were: Verizon; Cola-Cola; Cisco; Merck; Procter & Gamble, with prices ranging from $43.83 to $153.18.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of October 1 were: Chevron; Johnson & Johnson; Amgen; McDonald's; UnitedHealth, with prices ranging from $154.58 to $348.30.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the feckless Fed and Trade war-fueled slide gains momentum through 2025, with the exception of a very few Crypto and (maybe) Energy stocks, look for at least half of the ten Dow Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or quite suddenly.

Afterword

Buy One 'Safer' Dow October DiviDog; Watch Three More

Late September, 2025, 20 of the 30 Dow Index were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 10/1/25 data showed one was fair-priced.

How Dow 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price" Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that one dividend from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Dow dogs conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share. That one is Verizon Communications still standing after passing the 'safer' dividend gauntlet.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called "fair price."

A market correction of 83.9% is required to put all 10 "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or create stock splits to bring prices the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much "good" elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dogs of the Dow updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dow dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations. Just remember Verizon In October 2025 and the three stand-by Dow dogs of October: Cisco, Honeywell, and Procter & Gamble.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: RLJ Lodging: A Hybrid Approach With The High-Yielding Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

Although hotel REITs have to deal with rapidly changing circumstances, I still have long positions in the preferred equity of some of the hotel REITs. As discussed in my update on Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT), I still see a good risk/reward trade-off and RLJ Lodging Trust (NYSE:RLJ) isn’t any different. The REIT has only one series of preferred shares outstanding, and there is no risk of that preferred security being called anytime soon.

The preferred dividends are still comfortably covered

As you likely know, it doesn't make a lot of sense to value a real estate company based on the net income. That's why a REIT’s appeal should be judged based on the FFO (funds from operations) and AFFO (adjusted FFO).

As you can see below, RLJ Lodging Trust reported a total FFO of $69 million in the second quarter of this year, and this brought the total FFO in the first semester to just under $111 million. If you would look at the AFFO result, you see that slightly higher at almost 120 million in the first semester.

The image below also shows that this is already including the total cost of the preferred dividends, which came in at just over 12.5 million. This means the total pre-dividend AFFO was approximately $132M, which means that RLJ needed less than 10% of its H1 AFFO to cover the preferred dividends, and that is quite a healthy ratio.

That being said, you should be aware that in the United states the capital expenditures are not yet included in the adjusted FFO. We know RLJ plans to spend between 80 and 100 million on capital expenditures this year and the midpoint of that guidance is approximately $90M, which works out to $45M per semester.

We also know RLJ have been guiding for a full year adjusted FFO per share of $1.38-1.58 on a diluted basis. The midpoint of that guidance implies a $225M AFFO, and $250M before taking the preferred dividends into consideration. This confirms the anticipated payout ratio of around 10%.

If we would also deduct the $90M in maintenance capex, the pre-dividend and post-capex AFFO would come in at around $160M, of which just under 16% would be required to cover the preferred dividends, and that of course still is a payout ratio I feel very comfortable with.

As the balance sheet below shows, RLJ has approximately $2.6B in liabilities, while the net debt as of the end of June was approximately $1.85B excluding the impact of the restricted cash reserves.

This results in a LTV ratio of approximately 43% based on the book value of the properties. And as you can see below, this already includes just over $2B in accumulated depreciation. This means the LTV ratio based on the fair value of the assets is likely quite substantially lower than the 43% based on the book value.

Using the acquisition cost of the land and buildings (and excluding the acquisition cost of the furniture and fixtures), the LTV ratio based on the acquisition cost will drop to just below 35%.

The preferred shares: good value for money

The preferred shares issued by RLJ Lodging Trust are "busted," as the REIT has no option to call these preferred shares (RLJ.PR.A) at the traditional $25 issue price. The REIT is able to ret are the preferred shares by converting them into 0.2806 of its common shares, but this is only possible when the share price of the commons shares exceeds $89.09 for a period of 20 of any consecutive trading days. I consider this to be a "theoretical" possibility for RLJ, as there is no Realistic possibility to assume that the shares, which are currently trading at just over $7 per share, will indeed reach the $89 level.

The preferred shares have a fixed preferred dividend of $1.95 per year, and as I think a call is extremely unlikely, the appeal of an investment in the preferred shares should be based on the current yield. And at the current share price, the preferred dividend yield is approximately 7.6%, which I think is a very attractive value proposition from a risk/reward ratio perspective.

Investment thesis

I have a long position in the preferred shares of RLJ Lodging Trust, as I think the preferred shareholders are handsomely rewarded for the relatively low-risk given the robust financial results and the balance sheet, which contains a relatively low level of debt.

I’m also getting very interested in RLJ’s common shares. After all, the AFFO per share after taking capex into consideration will come in around $0.90, which makes the common stock pretty attractive as well. This means I should probably consider a hybrid approach.

data.title: PepsiCo, Levi Strauss Set To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

The stock market opened higher on Friday, as Wall Street seemed to remain unfazed by the U.S. government shutdown. Wall Street climbed higher on Thursday as both the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite and benchmark S&P 500 hit new intraday record highs as the government shutdown hit a second day. The shutdown came into effect at midnight on Wednesday after Republicans and Democrats failed to reach a federal funding deal. Wall Street has largely shrugged off the shutdown. On the prediction market Kalshi, the odds of the length of the shutdown lasting more than 10 days ticked up to 69%.

Amid a U.S. government shutdown, investors will keep a close eye on economic reports that will be released next week; however, government numbers are in doubt as the shutdown continues. The economic reports that are expected to be announced next week include the CB Employment Trends Index on Monday, with Exports, Imports, Trade Balance, Atlanta Fed GDP, and Consumer Credit on Tuesday. Wednesday will see the release of FOMC Minutes, MBA Mortgage Applications, Construction Spending, and Gasoline Production, with Initial and Continuing Jobless Claims and Wholesale Inventories on Thursday. Meanwhile, reports on Average Hourly Earnings, Unemployment Rate, Nonfarm Payrolls, and Michigan Consumer Sentiment are expected to be released on Friday.

Constellation Brands (STZ), PepsiCo (PEP), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Levi Strauss (LEVI), Tilray (TLRY), and Unity Bancorp (UNTY) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, October 6: Constellation Brands, Aehr Test Systems (AEHR). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, October 7: McCormick & Co (MKC), Saratoga Investment Corp (SAR). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, October 8: Richardson Electronics (RELL). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, October 9: PepsiCo, Delta Air Lines, Levi Strauss, Tilray. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, October 10: Unity Bancorp (UNTY). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) and Oklo (OKLO) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Tourmaline Bio (TRML), Vimeo (VMEO), and NovaGold Resources (NG). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include WhiteHorse Finance (WHF), Kodiak AI (KDK), and Oxford Square (OXSQ).

IPO watch: Alliance Laundry (ALH) is expected to price its IPO and begin to trade. The manufacturer of commercial laundry equipment based in Ripon, Wisconsin, plans to raise up to $751.2 million by offering roughly 34.1 million shares at a price range of $19 to $22 per share. Phoenix Education Partners (PXED) is also expected to start trading. The operator of the University of Phoenix aims to raise up to $140.3 million through an IPO of 4.25 million shares at a price range of $31 to $33 per share, which puts the post-IPO valuation at approximately $1.2 billion at the midpoint. The post-IPO quiet period ends for Via Transportation (VIA), Klarna Group (KLAR), Black Rock Coffee Bar (BRCB), Gemini Space Station (GEMI), and Figure Technology (FIGR) to free up analysts to post ratings.

PepsiCo Earnings Preview: PepsiCo is set to report its third-quarter earnings next week, and analysts expect the company to report earnings per share of $2.26 on revenue of $23.86B. The beverage giant is being closely watched as the company considers new strategies, with activist investor Elliott Management calling for strategic changes such as refranchising to improve bottling inefficiencies. Jefferies analyst Kaumil Gajrawala said a franchised model enables growth using the capital of others, including capital PepsiCo may not have or may prefer to deploy elsewhere. A move to refranchise could sharpen the focus on high-growth areas of the PepsiCo portfolio.

Levi Strauss Earnings Preview: Apparel giant Levi Strauss is scheduled to announce third-quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting earnings per share of $0.30 on revenue of $1.5B. Needham recently started off coverage on Levi Strauss & Co. with a Buy rating on its view that it is a compelling momentum story. Analyst Tom Nikic and his team believe the apparel giant is executing well on its strategic initiatives, which include omnichannel growth and category diversification, while also benefiting from the ongoing strong denim trend.
