data.title: My Top Pick For October Yields 14%: AGNC Investment
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

There's a well-known Wall Street adage that says, "Buy the rumor, sell the news."

Many investors were expecting interest rate-sensitive investments to immediately spike once interest rates were cut, if they were cut, and as such, a lot of interest rate-sensitive investments were climbing on the expectation of rate cuts to occur.

The Federal Reserve cut its target rate by 25 bps, with one dissenter who wanted to cut rates by 50 bps. Another rate cut is widely expected in October. While there is plenty of time for economic reports to change expectations, it is clear that the Federal Reserve is increasingly concerned about the labor market and believes that the risks of unemployment rising are higher than the risk of inflation.

Interestingly, after the rate cut decision, both the general market (SPX) as well as REITs (VNQ), which generally benefit from interest rate cuts, sold off. This isn't expected to be a long-term trend, but for the market itself, it meant that there was more information given in the update from the rate cut that concerned investors than the excitement over the rate cut itself. It also followed the very well-known adage: once the rate cut was news, people were selling, trying to lock in any gains they benefited from by buying the rumor ahead of time.

Today, I want to look at an investment that we have covered previously that benefits tremendously from rate cuts. These kinds of benefits, though, don't happen overnight. They happen over time, and we can expect those benefits to accumulate even more so as additional rate cuts occur, if they do.

Let's dive in!

I'm Still Buying Rate-Sensitive Opportunities

Interest-rate-sensitive stocks benefit from a dovish Fed, and there are few stocks that are more interest-rate-sensitive than agency mREITs like AGNC Investment Corp. (NASDAQ:AGNC), yielding 14.4%.

AGNC primarily invests in "agency MBS", which are mortgage-backed securities that are guaranteed by the agencies Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. If a borrower defaults, the agency buys the mortgage back at par value. Agency MBS typically trade with a very high correlation and at a relatively tight spread over US Treasuries, because they are considered very low risk from a credit perspective.

However, since mortgages can be prepaid whenever a borrower wants, and often are prepaid through refinancing, selling the house, or making extra payments, agency MBS does diverge from US Treasuries. If you buy a 30-year Treasury Bond, you know you won't be paid back until maturity in 30 years. If you buy a 30-year agency MBS, you can expect to get most of your money back in 5-7 years. The life expectancy of agency MBS can be projected, but it cannot be known because it is very dependent upon interest rate movements.

AGNC takes advantage of the difference by borrowing short-term using a form of lending known as repos. Repos are very low risk for the lenders because they have the right to the underlying collateral, in this case, agency MBS. Also, the contracts are typically less than 90 days, creating a very low duration risk for the lender. As a result, the lending rate on repos is typically very close to the Federal Reserve's target rate.

The risk is that AGNC's assets are MBS that can be expected to remain outstanding for several years. The risk to AGNC is that its cost of borrowing can exceed the yield it receives. AGNC's cash flow will be directly impacted by its average asset yield minus its cost of funds: Source

The Federal Reserve's target rate was above AGNC's average asset yield for a couple of years. Today, it is 4-4.25%, providing a comfortable cushion.

Yet if we look at AGNC's cost of funds, we can see that it is 2.86%, which is much lower than the Fed rate. This is caused by AGNC's hedging portfolio, specifically its interest-rate swaps. Contracts where AGNC agrees to pay a fixed interest rate and receives a floating interest rate. AGNC aggressively bought up swaps when interest rates were super low. Those swaps have been maturing, and we can see AGNC's cost of funds drift upward.

This directly caused AGNC's spread income per share to decrease over the past two years. In this chart, we can see AGNC's net interest spread (Average asset yield - cost of funds) and how it translated into AGNC's per/share earnings: Source

What does the recent Fed cut mean? Let's look at how "cost of funds" is calculated: Source

We can see that AGNC's repo costs were 4.44%. That number can be expected to come down very close to exactly as much as the Fed's target rate. So a 25 bps cut will result in that number coming down about 25 bps.

TBA (To Be Announced) are futures, where AGNC simultaneously buys and sells future contracts maturing in different months, with the "interest rate" being the implied financing costs of the transaction. That rate is impacted by numerous factors, including the expectation of future rate cuts.

Then, the benefit of interest rate swaps is subtracted to arrive at the average cost of funds. Note that AGNC had $45.8 billion in swaps hedging $59.4 billion in borrowings.

The Fed's 25 bps rate cut will immediately benefit the $13.6 billion in unhedged repo borrowings. 0.25% of $13.6 billion is $34 million in lower interest.

With the swapped portion, it is more complicated. AGNC has $8 billion in swaps where they are currently paying 0.17%, and those swaps are going to mature over Q3 and Q4: Source

If they don't replace those swaps, they will be paying about 4.19% on the underlying repo after the Fed's rate cut. That is better than 4.44%, but still a lot higher than the 0.17% they were paying. 4% interest on $8 billion is $320 million/year.

However, swap rates are also impacted by the expectation of Fed rate cuts. In the 5-10 year range, which is the sweet spot for agency mREITs, rates are currently 3.264%-3.541%. Source

Note, they were actually lower in 2024 when the market expected the Fed to be more aggressive with its rate-cutting cycle. AGNC will have the option of replacing its existing swap with a new one. If AGNC replaces the swaps, then they get a lower rate immediately than they are currently paying on their repo contracts. However, if the Fed does cut rates below 3.25-3.5%, then in the future they might be "overpaying".

On the other hand, AGNC can just allow the swaps to expire and allow their interest expense to be unhedged. This would result in an increase to 4.19% today, but if the Fed cuts in October, it will be 25 bps lower. And their expense would be reduced directly with each cut.

This is a decision that AGNC will need to make as its swaps expire. It is important to note that through the end of 2025, the swaps expiring are going to have a larger financial impact than repo rates coming down. As calculated above, repo rates coming down will be a benefit of roughly $34 million per 25 bps in rate cuts. If we assume three rate cuts by year's end, which is the consensus expectation, that would be roughly $100 million in savings.

On the other hand, the $8 billion in swaps that are paying only 0.17% now will be rolling off. If we assume 75 bps in repo cost reduction, they would still be paying 3.69% vs the current 0.17%, an increase of 352 bps. 3.52% of $8 billion is $281.6 million, or a $0.07/quarter headwind to earnings if AGNC goes completely unhedged. If AGNC chooses to enter into new swaps for some or all of the debt rolling off, the impact could be smaller.

So is the Fed rate cut good for AGNC's cash flow? Yes. It does ultimately lead to AGNC paying less interest expense. However, we need to be aware that we aren't going to see a huge spike in earnings from AGNC yet because they have these swaps maturing that have inflated earnings the past few years. That's an approximately $0.07/quarter headwind, where each 25 bps fed rate cut is a $0.007/quarter tailwind.

AGNC has other tailwinds. For example, it can raise common equity at a premium to book value and issue preferred equity, and buy more new MBS. On a hedged basis, AGNC can easily deploy newly raised capital and generate a return on equity that is higher than its current dividend. At a high level, this works by increasing AGNC's average asset yield, and higher revenues will also help offset the increase in interest expense as the swaps mature.

The bottom line is that declining rates are beneficial to AGNC, but we expect earnings in the near term will be relatively flat. We anticipate the tailwinds being generated by a lower Fed rate will primarily offset the maturity of the extremely favorable interest rate swaps, resulting in relatively flat earnings through the end of 2025. However, as we go into 2026 the pace of interest rate swap maturities is much slower, the swaps are already at a higher rate, and the Fed will have time to cut rates even more. As a result, the interest rate swaps that have restrained earnings growth in 2025 will be a much less relevant factor in 2026.

AGNC peers have been raising dividends, while AGNC has kept its dividends the same. This is largely because AGNC was already paying out a higher dividend relative to book value. The very attractive swaps it snagged in 2021 are a big reason why some peers had to cut dividends in 2022/2023, and AGNC didn't. The swaps managed to last long enough to make it to an environment where AGNC can support its dividend without them.

To the extent that AGNC can continue issuing equity over book value and grow in 2026, dividend raises could be on the table within the next few years.

Conclusion

For AGNC, the benefit of declining rates or interest rate cuts is not an instantaneous proposition. Likewise, if you were to take a 3-mile run today, you would not instantly wake up with a perfectly sculpted body tomorrow. No, the benefit is cumulative over time. The more you run, the more the benefit. The longer you run, the greater the benefit. Likewise, for AGNC, as rates remain lower than before and continue to decline, the benefits will accumulate over time.

For traders who buy the rumor and sell the news, AGNC is going to move quickly into the category of no longer meeting any of their interests. This year, AGNC has strongly outperformed the market on the expectation of interest rate cuts, not that they're here, we're seeing traders exit their positions, bringing current returns closer to that of the overall market – traders locking in short-term gains:

For income investors who position their portfolio to benefit regardless of what interest rates are doing, AGNC continues to be a highly attractive opportunity to collect double-digit yields paid out monthly from ultra-safe investments in agency MBS. AGNC itself does add risk by adding in leverage to the scenario and so it does not carry the same ultra-low risk rating that Agency MBS would on its own. However, it is well-positioned to continue to benefit from additional interest rate cuts, as well as benefit from the newer, lower rate that we've just seen.

When it comes to retirement, collecting wonderful income that pours in from the market to your coffers is a great idea. Rarely does anything in life come for free, and with every time you put money to work, there's going to be some risk that is tied to it. Even leaving money in a bank account has risk. Risk that the bank may collapse, risk that the government won't honor its FDIC insurance, and the loss of value over time due to the eroding effects of inflation. Hiding your money under a mattress also comes with a risk of theft. If that cash is stolen, you can kiss it goodbye, and inflation still negatively impacts it there. By putting your money to work in the market, you can help balance that risk with reward by having your money earn more and be able to achieve the retirement that you're dreaming of. Don't let your finances be what stops you from having the retirement that you've always dreamed of. That's where the unique Income Method within High Dividend Opportunities can massively benefit you by helping you unlock the potential of your nest egg.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Do Not Take Profits On PetroChina, Despite Its Spectacular Rally
data.description:
In early 2021, I recommended buying PetroChina (OTCPK:PCCYF) for its promising growth prospects and its cheap valuation. Since then, the stock has offered an eye-opening total return of 346%, and thus it has outperformed the S&P 500 (+77%) by an impressive margin. I reiterated my bullish thesis about six months later, with similar results since that article. Last year, I recommended holding PetroChina, as the stock was not as attractive as it was before the extraordinary rally, but it remained reasonably valued. I reiterated my hold rating about six months ago, as the cheap valuation of the stock mitigated its risk amid a deteriorating outlook of the oil market. Since that article, the stock has outperformed the S&P 500 by a wide margin, as it has offered a total return of 28%, whereas the index has rallied 17%.

After such vast outperformance, it is only natural that some investors may think that PetroChina has become overvalued. Given also a weak outlook of the oil market (more on this below), it is reasonable to consider taking profits after the exceptional rally of the stock. Nevertheless, the oil and gas producer maintains promising growth potential while it remains cheaply valued. Therefore, investors should resist the temptation to take their profits.

Business Overview

PetroChina passes under the radar of most U.S. investors, who are much more familiar with the domestic oil majors, such as Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX) and ConocoPhillips (COP). However, PetroChina is the second-largest producer of oil and gas in the world by volume, behind only Saudi Aramco (ARMCO). In the first half of this year, the Chinese oil giant produced an all-time high of 5.1 million barrels per day, which was 11% higher than the production of 4.6 million barrels per day of Exxon Mobil.

A reason for investors to be concerned over the entire energy sector is the weak outlook of the global oil market. OPEC provided strong support to the price of oil in recent years by cutting its production quotas aggressively. However, the U.S. and Canada, which do not belong to the cartel, have been growing their output, and thus they have been grasping market share from OPEC. Consequently, OPEC members became upset, seeing their sacrifices almost wasted.

This situation led OPEC to change its strategy drastically, just as it had done in 2014-2016, shortly after the onset of the shale oil boom. In that downturn of the oil industry, the price of oil had plunged approximately 70%, from $100 in mid-2014 to a bottom of $30 in early 2016, due to the supply glut caused by the shale oil boom and the ramp-up of production of OPEC. In the current situation, OPEC began to restore its production aggressively in April, aiming to grow its output by approximately 2.0 million barrels per day by the end of next year.

The ramp-up of the output of OPEC has caused a huge oil surplus in the global oil market. According to the latest report of the Energy Information Administration [EIA], global oil inventories are expected to increase by more than 2.0 million barrels per day from the third quarter of this year through the first quarter of 2026. The global oil surplus is expected to somewhat moderate later in 2026, but only thanks to a significant decline in oil prices, which is likely to lead oil producers to reduce their output. Due to the supply glut of oil, EIA expects the average price of Brent to decrease 25%, from $68 in 2025 to $51 in 2026. This is certainly a strong headwind for most oil producers.

On the bright side, PetroChina has been growing its production meaningfully for years and still has promising growth prospects. The Chinese giant has grown its output by about 3% per year on average over the last four years, from 4.45 million barrels of oil in 2021 to 5.07 million barrels of oil in the first half of this year. It also has promising growth potential thanks to a series of growth projects.

Source: Investor Presentation

In the first half of 2025, PetroChina proved resilient to the slowdown of the oil industry. The average realized price of oil decreased 15% over the prior year’s period, but the company grew its production 2% while it also reduced its cost of production by 9%. As a result, its total operating profit dipped only 7%. It is also worth noting that the oil and gas producer achieved all-time high production in the first half of the year.

The total operating profit of PetroChina was supported, not only by the increased output and the reduced cost of production, but also by the fast-growing natural gas marketing business of the company.

Source: Investor Presentation

As shown in the above chart, the domestic volumes of natural gas have grown by 10% in total in the last two years. Given also a boost from higher gas prices, the gross profit of this segment surged 67% from the first half of 2023 to the first half of 2025.

China has been growing its consumption of natural gas at a fast pace over the last 25 years and is likely to continue doing so, as the country is trying to somewhat improve its environmental footprint. The sustained growth of demand for natural gas in China is likely to remain a strong growth driver for PetroChina.

Valuation

PetroChina has posted earnings per share of $0.12 in the last 12 months, and thus it is currently trading at a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 7.7x. This earnings multiple is much lower than the median trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 12.4x of the energy sector. On the one hand, as a Chinese company, PetroChina is perceived as somewhat riskier than its U.S. peers due to the inherent risk related to the lack of transparency and unpredictability that characterize the Chinese government. On the other hand, the depressed valuation of PetroChina provides a margin of safety amid the aforementioned weakening outlook of the global oil market. Overall, the cheap valuation of PetroChina mitigates the risk of the stock and is likely to highly reward in the future those who purchase the stock around its current price.

Final Thoughts

When the outlook of a sector deteriorates, it is usually best to avoid the entire sector. However, PetroChina has proved fairly resilient so far and is likely to remain resilient, primarily thanks to the strong growth of its natural gas business. In addition, its cheap valuation provides some protection in the event of business deterioration. Therefore, its shareholders should not rush to take their profits, despite the extraordinary rally of the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American States Water: Scoop Up This Dividend King Now
data.description:
The Dividend Kings are among the most elite stocks in the dividend growth stock universe. Why do we make this claim?

Think about the long and arduous path to becoming a Dividend King. Raising a dividend for at least 50 consecutive years implies that a company will have to endure at least a handful of full business cycles.

Plenty of periods of economic growth. And some economic contraction sprinkled into the mix.

The latter times are what make Dividend Kings so impressive. Weathering economic downturns is the true test for dividend growth stocks.

This is what brings us to our focus for today, which is American States Water (NYSE:AWR). One underappreciated fact about this company is its reputation for dividend growth.

Initially, this seems counterintuitive. Isn't AWR a Dividend King?

Yes, but its distinction as a dividend growth stock runs even deeper.

Not only is AWR simply a Dividend King. It's the Dividend King. To be more precise, AWR is the longest-reigning Dividend King.

On July 31st, AWR upped its quarterly dividend per share by 8.3% to $0.504. This marked the 71st consecutive year that the regulated utility upped its payout.

When we last covered AWR with a buy rating in March, this fact wasn't lost on us. We also thought the company was well-positioned for future EPS growth. AWR's A-rated balance sheet was another positive attribute. Lastly, shares looked to be moderately undervalued.

Six months later, we're maintaining our buy rating. AWR is set to meaningfully invest in infrastructure for the foreseeable future. The company also recently completed a transaction with a developer for a newly planned community. AWR remains a financial fortress. Solidifying our buy case is the fact that shares could be a marginally better value than they were in March.

AWR Should Have More Growth Ahead

On August 6th, AWR released its earnings report for the second quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The company's total operating revenue grew by 5% year-over-year to $163.1 million in the quarter. For more perspective, this missed Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus by $7.6 million during the quarter.

In our view, AWR's topline miss wasn't for any concerning reasons.

The factor that accounts for total operating revenue coming up short had to do with its Contracted Services segment for the second quarter. The segment's operating revenue declined by 15.9% over the year-ago period to $30.4 million in the quarter. The reason behind this decreased operating revenue was a decrease in construction activities. That was simply due to timing, which was partially countered by higher management fees stemming from economic price adjustments.

Growth from AWR's Water and Electric segments more than made up for the lower Contracted Services segment revenue.

The former segment showed an 8.4% increase in revenue to $119.7 million during the second quarter. This was due to higher 2025 rates approved by the California Public Utilities Commission that went into effect in January, as well as greater water consumption.

AWR's Electric segment revenue surged 48.5% higher year-over-year to $30.4 million for the second quarter. That was influenced by third-year rate increases implemented in 2025, which were more than the 2022 rates used to record revenue in Q2 2024.

AWR's diluted EPS edged 2.4% higher over the year-ago period to $0.87 in the second quarter. This came in $0.04 under Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter.

Looking to the future, AWR's fundamentals appear to be respectable.

The key growth driver for the company continues to be its infrastructure investments. For the three years through 2027, AWR plans to invest $648.7 million in its water and electric operations. This should help the utility to continue growing its rate base at a healthy clip.

Additionally, AWR closed a transaction with a developer for water and wastewater system assets in a development located in the California Central Coast region.

According to CEO and President Robert Sprowls' opening remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call, this newly planned community will serve up to roughly 1,300 customers at full build-out. This is expected to happen by 2034 under the current construction schedule. The company already began serving a few customers in Q2 2025 in connection with this transaction.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is encouraging for AWR. In 2025, diluted EPS is expected to grow by 5.1% to $3.33. For 2026, diluted EPS is forecasted to increase by 4.5% to $3.48. In 2027, an additional 7.5% boost in diluted EPS to $3.74 is anticipated.

AWR is financially sound, too. The company's interest coverage ratio through the first six months of 2025 was 4.7. This points to a highly solvent business. That's why AWR possesses an A S&P credit rating with a stable outlook (unless otherwise noted or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was from AWR's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release, AWR's Q2 2025 10-Q Filing, and AWR's Q2 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value Is Above $85 A Share

Since our last article in March, shares of AWR have been relatively flat. All the while, much of our forward 12-month diluted EPS input has moved from 2025 into 2026.

This has arguably made the water utility's shares even more attractive. To this point, the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 21.1. That's roughly three and a half standard deviations beneath the 10-year average P/E ratio of 32.3, per FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, we believe that fair value for AWR remains about two standard deviations less than the 10-year average P/E ratio. Put another way, our fair value P/E ratio is right around 25.

AWR's growth profile looks to be holding up. The annual diluted EPS growth consensus of 5.9% is within the margin of error of its 10-year average growth rate of 7.3%.

As was the case in March, the sole factor working against AWR's valuation is the forward-looking interest rate environment. The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is currently 4.1%. This is far higher than the 10-year average yield of 2.5%, according to Macrotrends.

That implies AWR's earnings yield investment spread to the 10-year Treasury has been under 75 basis points. Since the company's fundamentals are intact, we expect a minimal spread to continue. Applying our expectations of a 10-year yield in the high-3% range in the coming years, this supports our fair value P/E ratio of 25.

As of today, the calendar year 2025 is 75% complete. That suggests there is another 25% of 2025 and 75% of 2026 still to come over the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month diluted EPS input of $3.44.

Using our fair value multiple of 25, we get a fair value of $86 a share. Relative to the current $73 share price (as of September 30th, 2025), that's equivalent to a 16% discount to fair value.

If AWR matches growth forecasts and returns to fair value, it could deliver a 23% upside through September 2026. By the end of 2030, the utility could post 11% annual total returns.

AWR Continues To Deliver Impressive Dividend Growth

AWR's 2.8% forward dividend yield comes in about 70 basis points below the 3.5% median forward yield of the utility sector. This is an approximately 20 basis point narrowing in the spread versus March. That accounts for the D+ and D grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield from Seeking Alpha's Quant System, respectively (up from D and D- in March).

Relative to its sector, AWR's starting income is certainly underwhelming. However, the utility is truly exceptional in every other aspect.

For starters, AWR has 71 consecutive years of dividend growth under its belt. This is believed to be the most established track record of dividend growth among U.S. publicly traded companies. That dwarfs the sector median dividend growth streak of just 10.4 years. This is why the Quant System issues an A+ grade to AWR for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from March).

As we outlined earlier, the utility also just upped its quarterly dividend per share by 8.3% in late July. That's relatively similar to the 10-year average annual dividend growth rate of 8.2%. This implies that dividend growth isn't slowing down at all.

Looking ahead, the Quant System anticipates 8.4% annual forward dividend per share growth. This would be well above the sector median of merely 5.3%. That's also consistent with the 7%+ annual dividend growth that the company is targeting. This is sufficient for AWR to earn a B+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (unchanged from March).

What's more, the utility's dividend is especially safe. AWR's diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to register in the high-50% range for 2025. That would be comfortably less than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry, according to the Zen Research Terminal.

This is why AWR has the lowest dividend cut risk among utilities in the Zen Research Terminal. Over the next 12 months, the risk of a dividend cut is estimated to be just 0.1%. In the next average recession, that risk remains subdued at only 0.75%. The Quant System also awards a B+ grade to AWR for overall dividend safety, which is arguably a conservative grade (unchanged from March).

Risks To Consider

In terms of dividend growth, AWR is the best of the very best. Yet, the company does face risks that could weigh on its investment thesis if they materialize.

The risk that remains top of mind is AWR's considerable operating presence in California. Through the first six months of 2025, the utility generated 80.2% of its total operating revenue in the Golden State (page 10 of 78 of AWR's Q2 2025 10-Q Filing). The remainder was generated at military base locations throughout the United States.

On the one hand, the most recent rate case was reasonably constructive for the company. This does show that CPUC is willing to work with investor-owned utilities. On the other hand, this could change in the future.

The catalyst that could be an agent of change would be political pressure. A recent AARP survey of Americans 50 and older found that 83% of those surveyed were concerned about an increase in the cost of their electricity service. That could lead to more pushback from regulators across the country for various utility services.

In a worst-case scenario, this could prompt credit rating agencies to downgrade AWR's credit ratings. This could reduce growth investments and hurt the company's growth profile.

The other risk stemming from AWR's geographic concentration in California is from a natural disaster standpoint. Since 1953, the Golden State has had 370 Federal Emergency Management Agency natural disaster declarations, per World Population Review. This trails only the 372 natural disaster declarations of Texas.

One final risk to AWR is the potential for a significant cyber breach. Such an event could interrupt the company's operations and compromise sensitive customer data. The aftermath of this type of incident could include costly litigation and penalties imposed by regulators.

Summary: A Buyable SWAN Stock

AWR is the epitome of a world-class business.

The company's continued investments in infrastructure should produce mid- to high-single-digit annual diluted EPS in the years ahead. AWR has a balance sheet that supports these investments, too.

The utility's beginning dividend income is modest. Yet, AWR compensates for this with phenomenal dividend consistency, growth, and safety.

Further supporting our buy rating is the stock's double-digit percentage discount to our fair value estimate. This is why we're standing by our buy rating.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending: Buy The Dip Opportunity
data.description:
Morgan Stanley Direct Lending's (NYSE:MSDL) shareholders (including me) didn't have a pleasant experience with its stock price declining by ~20% YTD. So far, I've only published bullish articles about MSDL and some may wonder if such a poor performance left me wondering if it's time to let go and sell.

Let me state right from the get-go, I think MSDL's stock price decline went far over the line. At this point, MSDL trades at a substantially lower multiple than its very close peer - Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). With the most recent NAV taken into account, MSDL trades at ~0.8x while BXSL (which is very comparable based on the investing strategy, asset mix, and KPIs) trades at nearly 1.0x. Does MSDL deserve to trade at the same level as BXSL? Not necessarily, but the valuation gap is too significant.

Therefore, I've decided to upgrade my previous ratings to "strong buy". Even with struggling dividend coverage, MSDL's income potential remains attractive. I don't think it deserves to trade at a 20% discount to its NAV, so on top of the income potential I expect upside from the multiple appreciation.

However, it's not that simple... Let's review MSDL's situation, upside potential, and risk factors accompanying the Company.

MSDL is not a risk-free investment

When we look at MSDL in a nutshell, it's a debt-oriented BDC with a heavy focus on first-lien debt investments, which provide the highest margin of safety for capital-providers when compared to any other form of debt. With 99.6% of MSDL's loans structured as floating-rated loans, MSDL is naturally very susceptible to interest rate changes.

Since the FED started to decrease rates last year and this process continues until now, MSDL's ability to generate net investment income decreased. Yes, there are some factors that somewhat offset this trend such as access to cheaper debt or more active transactional market. Still, these factors prove to be insufficient from the perspective of MSDL's NII.

For instance, in Q2 2024 MSDL's NII amounted to $0.63 per share, while the last available data showed $0.50 regarding Q2 2025. It's worth noticing that with MSDL's quarterly regular dividend amounting to $0.50 per share, the regular dividend coverage is at concerning 100% level, which should spark caution among investors.

Although the management shared optimistic remarks about MSDL's most recent performance and its ability to uphold dividend payments, I recommend taking it with a grain of salt. Quoting from the last Earnings Call:

In terms of operating results, we generated net investment income of $0.50 per share, in line with the $0.50 dividend declared. Our high-quality earnings in the second quarter were driven by continued stability in the underlying credit performance of the portfolio and were characterized by low and declining contributions from payment in kind and other income. We remain comfortable with our distribution level, supported by a normalization in asset yields and due to the progress that we have continued to make on optimizing the right-hand side of the balance sheet.

Are there any good news? Take a look at the dividend yield. If you were to assume that MSDL will continue paying $0.50 per share quarterly, it would currently yield ~12%, so even when faced with the some dividend cuts, MSDL will still serve you as a productive income source.

What's worth mentioning as another positive of lower interest rate environment is the fact that lower cost of debt improves the credit quality of portfolio companies. MSDL's non-accruals look solid with just 0.7% ratio at cost recorded as of June 30, 2025. If you look at the table below, you will surely notice that 1-rated investments' share in MSDL's portfolio has increased over the last couple of quarters and currently stands 2.3%. A the same time, the combined share of 3 and 4-rated investments stands at 1.7% (same as in Q4 2024). While the value of 4-rated investments has increase slightly, it's not a major issue from the perspective of MSDL's portfolio value.

Conclusions

With quite a short trading history, MSDL unfortunately didn't provide great performance in terms of its stock price behaviour. At the same time, I think the market has gone too far with that and is currently underappreciating MSDL.

The Company has potential to not only provide meaningful income but also experience multiple appreciation that could allow it to reduce the valuation gap between MSDL and its close peer - BXSL. I own both BDCs and don't consider them as different as to accept such a substantial valuation gap.

With first-lien debt oriented portfolio and low non-accruals, MSDL is well-positioned to serve as your income source. No, it's not certain that MSDL has the ability to uphold the level of regular dividends it currently presents, but even when accounted for the risk of a dividend cut, the income potential is still there.

Therefore, I expect MSDL's stock price to increase by ~10% to reduce the valuation gap between its 0.8x P/NAV and BXSL's 1.0x P/NAV. After combining that with a double-digit dividend yield, MSDL presents a "strong buy" opportunity. However, one should mind expectations around its ability to uphold dividend payments at current levels if interest rates continue to decline. Cheers!

data.title: Scotiabank: Fundamentals Justify A Buy Rating, But Technicals Suggest Some Caution
data.description:
In just three months after my last coverage, The Bank of Nova Scotia, or simply Scotiabank, surged by almost 20%. This justifies the upside potential I saw previously, which adhered to my buy rating. Its fundamentals are still well-positioned and solid despite volatile market conditions. Technicals adhere to this as bullish signals remain evident. Yet, investors may wish to be more cautious due to the current overbuying, which may lead to profit-taking and price dips. This appears in line with valuation, as upside appears quite tricky today.

Scotiabank: Why It’s Still So Strong

Despite macroeconomic volatility, The Bank of Nova Scotia remains strongly insulated against headwinds. These are the great things about its business.

1. The loan base is still solid. Amid the rate cut pause, BNS still struggles with lower yields on interest-sensitive items like credit cards and personal loans. Yet, it enjoys higher confidence among borrowers. So, it’s not surprising that its loan volume remains relatively stable with a slight increase.

With respect to its consumer-corporate loan exposure. Corporate loan exposure increased from 38% in my previous coverage to 44% despite lower government and business loans. This was mainly due to the increased CRE loan volume. In my view, this is crucial in ensuring low delinquencies since these entities are typically more capable of making timely repayments and are collateralized.

As we can see, 42% of its CRE loans come from residential or multi-family properties. While returns are low due to supply-demand imbalances, it still enjoys the highest asset value in the US at $4.61T. The second-largest portion of 23% comes from industrial properties. Currently, this has an asset value of $2.91T compared to only $2.24T before the pandemic. With that, it’s not surprising that its returns remain positive and more stable than office and multi-family properties.

Moreover, I think its loan base is still well-positioned today. Note that its credit quality remains relatively flat on a YoY basis. It also improved noticeably from Q1 and Q2 2025. I also believe that BNS can still keep loan delinquencies low despite the increase in personal loan and credit card volume. This can be supported by the Fed and BoC rate cut of 25 bps to 4.25% and 2.5% last month. This means lower borrowing costs and higher repayment capacity. In addition, rate cuts may help improve labor market conditions in the US, which can bolster investments and borrowings.

2. Fixed/Variable-Rate Loan management is in line with the recent rate cut and current interest rate trajectory. BNS remains concentrated on fixed-rate loans today. This is most evident in its residential mortgages. Currently, its variable-rate mortgages only comprise 34% of the total amount, while fixed-rate mortgages take the remaining 66%. While the percentage of variable-rate loans rose from 33% to 34%, we must note that the loan base also increased from $305M to $306M. In reality, the relative amount did not really change. This current loan split may work to BNS’s advantage for several reasons.

First, this split will insulate BNS from the unfavorable impact of the recent Fed and BoC rate cut. After all, variable rates are highly sensitive to interest rate hikes or cuts. With that, it’s not surprising that its interest income on loans remains relatively stable on a QoQ basis. In addition, its variable-rate spread over five years remains in line with the current macroeconomic conditions. For instance, only 13% of its maturing mortgages this FY 2025 have variable rates. But in 2026 and 2027, their portion will increase, which will be ideal due to potential economic recovery in those years. Interest rates are expected to be more stable by that time. In addition, there may be more economic activity.

3. A well-Positioned Balance Sheet Means More Returns. On a broader view, we can see that its whole Balance Sheet is healthy. One aspect is its decent loan-to-deposit ratio of 81%. This means that only 81% of the deposits are being lent out. BNS has excess liquidity or reserves, positioning it very well against macroeconomic disruptions. It will still cover its operating capacity in case of an increase in loan delinquencies. So, it can protect its business against deposit runs.

Aside from that, BNS has multiple income streams and does not heavily rely on loans. If we look at its interest-earning assets, 56% are loans, 21% are investment securities, 6% are cash and deposits, and 17% are other interest-earning assets.

This can help it become less sensitive to interest rate cuts and manage its yields. As we can see, interest income on loans, securities, and deposits dropped. Yet, interest income on other securities increased by CAD 304M YoY and CAD 56M QoQ to CAD 717M. It is also worth noting that about 40% of all its assets are liquid assets, such as cash, deposits, and investment securities.

4. Well-Capitalized Business Promises Liquidity And Dividend Longevity. Nothing much has changed in BNS with respect to its capital adequacy. Its CET1 ratio remains high at 13.3%. If we add the remaining high-quality capital, the Tier 1 Ratio is 15.2%. If we add the remaining capital, it will be 16.9%, which is still more than twice the Basel III requirement of at least 8.0%. The TLAC ratio is still very high at 29.0%. All these numbers prove that BNS can survive a recession or financial crisis without borrowing. It can prevent or at least reduce the risk of deposit runs.

Valuation: Looks Quite Expensive? The Market Doesn't Seem To Mind

After the price surged by almost 20%, I decided to revisit its valuation. Using P/B, the stock now trades at 1.34x its book value per share versus the five-year average of 1.19x. If we use this to derive its fair value, the TP will be $57.96. Clearly, the TP decreased because the stock price rose faster and larger than the book value per share. To confirm this, I also used P/S, which shows that the stock trades at 3.54x its sales per share versus the average of 3.14x. Using this, the TP will be $57.82.

Both price ratios justify the uptrend from my previous coverage. However, the actual price increase seems excessive. Despite this, we should note that price ratios are still historically driven since they only compare how cheap or expensive BNS is relative to previous years. And as we can see, the stock price has always been reactive to book value and sales per share. This can tell us that the market does not seem to care if the stock price increases faster than in previous years. As long as these financial metrics increase, the stock price will most likely increase as well.

To prove this, I also did a regression analysis and saw their correlation of 88%. This means that if either of the two metrics increases, there is an 88% probability that the price will increase. This is supported by their p-values since they show that less than 5% of their relationship happens randomly or by chance. But sales per share and price appear to have more consistency since their p-value is only 0.2%, which means very low outliers or volatile numbers. Hence, sales per share is a better predictor of the price trend. Regardless of the relative valuation, the stock price uptrend is justified as long as revenues increase.

I also updated its dividend discount model due to the changes in the risk-free rate from 4.2% to 4.1%. Annualized dividends also decreased from $3.24 to $3.16. But dividend growth remained the same at 3.5%. Using this, the TP will still be almost the same at $56.83. At this point, the price appears to be expensive relative to its dividends. The only consolation is that dividend yield remains high at nearly 5.0%, so I think BNS is still attractive. Nonetheless, I would like to signal caution considering its valuation.

Technicals

BNS’s bullish signals remain strong as the price increases with higher highs and lows above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. The 50-day SMA is also trending upward above the 200-day SMA, so recent market sentiments are still optimistic. However, it appears to have been consolidating in the past two weeks, so caution is crucial for traders.

Its MACD and RSI can explain the potential price consolidation. As we can see, the MACD line is decreasing below the signal line, which shows decreasing buying volume and some profit-taking. This is in line with the histogram within negative territory, which means that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Meanwhile, RSI can make things clearer as the potential dip is driven by the recent overbuying. We can see that it exceeded 90 before momentum slightly weakened. Until now, the stock is still overbought. So, there is a possibility for a dip once profit-takers act on it again.

Conclusion

The Bank of Nova Scotia remains well-positioned with its robust fundamentals, driven by prudent loan management and strategic asset diversification. It also stays highly profitable and liquid, which promises sustainability and dividend longevity. With respect to valuation, the stock remains reasonable relative to dividends and the trajectory of revenues and book value. However, upside may become limited. This can be verified by its current technicals. Momentum remains very bullish, but overbuying may weaken buying volume and lead to some dips. At this point, a buy rating still fits BNS, but I advise more caution for investors.

data.title: AppLovin Is Positioned To Capitalize On Ad-Tech With AXON
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my initial coverage on AppLovin Corporation (NASDAQ:APP), the stock has increased by 92% as of writing this. And today, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating. This is because the stock represents one of the most powerful combinations of growth and profitability in the adtech space. The reason I say this is because, when I look at the Quant Factor grades for the company, I note that the company has been improving in its overall grades in the last 3 months, as shown below. For instance, growth and profitability have remained unchanged at A+, while momentum and revisions have improved from A to A+ and A- to A.

In my previous coverage, I spoke about how I was watching the company since it was trading in the $40s level. And today, the stock is trading at $683 as of writing this. Despite the stock run in the last 2 years, there is still quite a lot to like about this company. Even from the mere look of quant grades, one is able to deduce that APP is quite a strong company positioned to rise to greater heights.

So, what are some of the reasons that make me believe that AppLovin warrants a Strong Buy rating?

First and foremost, as I have briefly mentioned, the numbers keep impressing every quarter. If you compare the Quant factor grades, you will realize that the numbers have been gradually improving over the last 6 months. In fact, in Q2 2025, revenues increased by 77% y/y to $1.26Bn while the adjusted EBITDA nearly doubled to $1.02Bn. This represents an 81% margin. For context, Meta's (META) EBITDA margin stands at 52.7% and operating margins at 43%, while Alphabet's (GOOGL)(GOOG) stand at 37.92% and operating margins at 33%, as per Quant ratings from SA. This means that APP is running at almost 2x the profitability of giants that dominate the global digital advertising space.

For sure these are incredible margins. And the best part is that free cash flows [FCFs] are scaling alongside $786 Mn as per the Q2 2025 Earnings Report. Yet, this is after recording $826Mn in Q1 2025. So the bottom-line is that this is a business that is minting cash while its growing faster than anyone else in the space.

Then secondly, is that AppLovin is no longer just a mobile gaming ad network. In fact, that label massively understates what the company has become. As the CEO puts it;

...We exist for one reason and one reason alone, help an advertiser, find a new customer, engage that customer, run an advertising campaign from top of funnel, all the way to bottom of funnel, get a conversion and price it on revenue. And that is a really, really hard thing to do...

Adam Foroughi, CEO during the 2025 Goldman Sachs Communicopia & Technology Conference

Clearly what Adam Foroughi is trying to say is that APP is now a full funnel with an AI driven advertising engine. Not a niche gaming ad player. So evidently, gaming was the proof of concept. And now APP is expanding into e-commerce, retail and eventually the wider web with new verticals.

The third reason is the technological moat that APP has. AXON is not a buzzword. It's a proper ML system that has been built for over a decade now. And it is now processing billions of impressions daily. Actually, the more data it ingests, the more predictive it becomes. The CFO, Matt Stumpf explained that APP is comfortable guiding between 20% and 30% baseline growth just from reinforcement learning alone. And this clearly implies that even without opening new categories, the model will keep compounding itself.

...reinforcement learning, which is super important in these AI technologies that the model continues to improve from the recommendations, the predictions that it's doing about what's going to happen when it shows an impression and then also directed model enhancements, so improvements to the model over time.

Matt Stumpf, CFO during the 2025 Goldman Sachs Communicopia & Technology Conference

Lastly, I would say it is the self-service rollout. The reason why I say this is the fact that traditionally, digital advertisement required a lot of hand holding and some level of understanding about ad targeting, geography, and demographics. Now, with Axon Ad Manager, any business of any size anywhere in the world can plug into the system. And clearly comes with so many benefits, as explained by Adam Foroughi:

...our self- service portal, which will serve as the foundation for our next decade of growth. Our ads manager has many benefits. It puts day-to- day controls directly in advertisers' hands, reducing friction. It enables credit card billing, eliminating the hassle of monthly invoicing. It provides the architecture for agents that can eventually automate every workflow. It establishes the framework for automatically generated ads. It simplifies onboarding through our recently launched Shopify app. It deepens integrations with attribution providers, giving customers more accurate reporting...

Adam Foroughi, CEO during Q2 2025 Earnings Call

I find this a key driver of growth because large companies like Alphabet and Meta used the same playbook to scale into tens of millions of advertisers. And now, APP is pulling that lever. In fact, the company just opened the platform to major global markets:

...we have 1 billion-plus users and they're not gamers...

...The vast majority of our user audience is outside the U.S. We will be releasing almost all markets once we go into this October 1 release.

Adam Foroughi, CEO during Q2 2025 Earnings Call

And lastly, the valuation looks reasonable relative to growth. Inasmuch as APP trades at a TTM GAAP P/E of 96.76x, its TTM GAAP PEG is below 1.0x. And this means that the market isn't pricing in the growth it's delivering. I will discuss more on this in the valuation section.

Growth Drivers

In my previous APP coverage, I said that 90% of commercial leaders expect to utilize gen AI solutions over the next two years, as per a McKinsey report. I also said that AI is transforming advertising, especially with AI optimized models. And what makes AXON very powerful is AI, which acts as its multiplier. Remember, Axon is a recommendation model based on reinforcement learning. And therefore every impression that is fed into the system becomes training data. In simple terms, the more data it gets, the smarter it becomes. Therefore, AI will become a key standard in the next couple of years, Axon will also become better and smarter.

I also note that Gaming Core still has room in APP. Remember, APP started out as a gaming platform. And clearly, gaming built the foundation that APP has today. So, with new categories, the base platform will still be expanding. In fact, the CFO explained that gaming alone can sustain between 20% to 30% growth y/y. And this is because every improvement in the model makes recommendations better and smarter, and this translates to more advertisement volume, which then ends up in profitability for the consumers and the company as well.

And lastly, e-commerce. I would say this will unlock a huge potential for APP. In late 2024, APP launched its pilot with hundreds of e-commerce advertisers. And by Q1 2025, the ramp had produced a billion-dollar run rate. And the goal is very clear, as put by the CEO:

...Once AXON is fully open next year, we plan to begin paid marketing to recruit new advertisers, which will drive predictable compounding growth...

And this only means that the funnel is about to widen significantly.

Valuation

First of all, I note that the company has maintained a strong A+ growth grade. FWD revenue growth stands at 31% against the sector median of 7.48%. And remember, this is an improvement on the previous article, where the FWD revenue growth rate stood at 26%. Clearly, Q2 2025 gave the FWD revenue growth rates a boost.

In terms of valuation, as earlier stated, the TTM GAAP PEG ratio stands at 0.47x, while the sector median stands at 1.03x. This shows that the stock is trading at a 53% discount relative to the sector. And the bottom line is that the market has not factored in APP's full growth story in its stock price.

In terms of peer analysis, I note that APP has a lower TTM PEG ratio than all the peers except for Criteo (CRTO) as shown below. And this clearly shows that APP has quite a lot to still give.

AppLovin's Key Risk

Similar to my previous coverage, I still believe that the biggest risk that APP now faces is the cyclical nature of the advertising industry. In most cases, unfavorable macroeconomic conditions could hurt APP's revenues. This is in the sense that most companies allocate marketing and advertising budgets on a yearly basis. And in most cases, during an economic downturn, marketing budgets are usually the first ones to get trimmed. Even with a superior ROI, APP is not immune.

Final Thoughts

With Q2 2025 numbers out, I am more confident that APP warrants a Strong Buy. Clearly, AI is here, and it's here to stay. And the AXON engine is no longer a hype but rather a proven AI-driven system that has already been tested with gaming. And the success rate was quite impressive to the point that it is now breaking into e-commerce and the broader web. The self-service rollout is also a big unlock for APP. And for these reasons, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: A Speculative Buy
data.description:
Overall Thesis

In this article, I will explain—using back-of-the-envelope math and plain English—why the stock of Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VKTX), one of the frontrunners in the GLP-1 weight-loss drug market, may represent a (relatively) de-risked investment opportunity with the promise of a speculative return, at least for long-term investors who have the cool head to see through the critical nuances and the stomach to watch a position wither by 40% in a single day.

At the outset, here is what I think the proverbial Mr. Market keeps missing: Viking does not need to deliver a first-in-class or best-in-class weight-loss drug to be worth multiples of today’s price. At around a $3 billion valuation, the stock is implicitly priced for the unlikely scenario in which each of its three real shots on goal—injectable VK2735, oral VK2735, and VK2809—fails to achieve meaningful market share, by, among other things, failing their respective pivotal clinical trials. I view that scenario as theoretically possible but practically improbable. In short, the risk-adjusted potential of the company as a whole far exceeds the current market capitalization of about $3 billion.

Let us look at the big picture and then dive into the critical nuances.

The Big Picture

Viking runs a two-front strategy. On one front is VK2735, a dual GLP-1/GIP agonist in both injectable form (currently in Phase III clinical trial) and oral form (which completed Phase II clinical trial late summer), aimed squarely at obesity, the largest therapeutic opportunity in modern pharma, backed up by amylin agonist, also for obesity, whose IND is planned for 3Q 2025. Those agonists rest on practically tested and validated biology, and both have shown meaningful efficacy (and, in the case of VK2735, potentially best-in-class efficacy).

On the other front is VK2809, a selective TR-β agonist for MASH, which reported successful Phase 2b clinical trial results in 2Q 2024, and VK0214, a selective TR-β agonist for X-AKD, which reported successful Phase 1b clinical trial results in 4Q 2024.

Despite the 40% crash in the share price following the report of Phase 2 oral VK2735 clinical results in August, 2025—driven primarily by concerns over the high discontinuation rates—both VK2735 injectable and VK2735 oral have met their Phase II primary and secondary endpoints.

Mr. Market has apparently assigned the appropriate value to the following fact: even if the oral form of VK2735 fails to be approved and marketed as a standalone weight-loss drug, tolerability should be much better for patients who start on sub-q VK2735 and then transition to oral for weight-loss maintenance, because both forms use the same molecule. Consequently, VK2735 will probably emerge at least as a treatment regimen in which patients begin with powerful and safe sub-q VK2735 and thereafter take oral VK2735 for maintenance, if oral VK2735, in the unlikely event, does not take off on its own as a standalone oral agent for weight loss.

This differentiator—and the resulting competitive advantage—should at least land Viking in the position where Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly may be the Coke and Pepsi of the weight loss business, while Viking is the Dr. Pepper. Viking does not need to own the entire soda aisle to build an enduring and lucrative weight-loss franchise. It just needs to succeed in one of the several clinical trials, obtain FDA approval for one of the several drug candidates, and capture a reasonable slice of a massive market to support its current $3 billion market cap.

What Mr. Market Has Missed

The VENTURE-Oral Phase 2 data dealt bulls like myself a 40% single-day drawdown on August 19, 2025. The bearish readout went roughly as follows: “Despite efficacy, discontinuations were high (treatment ~28% vs. 18% placebo), and Lilly looks better on most metrics; Lilly’s dropout in the mid-20% range is over 72 weeks, not 13. So Viking is not a big-time player near term.” (Mizuho, summarized)

The more bullish readout was closer to this: “At 13 weeks, the 60/90/120 mg arms delivered ~7.5%, 10%, and 11% placebo-adjusted weight loss—strong versus other orals. Tolerability was worse than expected from Phase 1 but overall manageable. Oral VK2735 still offers strong efficacy with a manageable tolerability profile.” (JPMorgan, summarized)

Let’s try to reconcile the apparent conflicts between them. We will find from the “VK2735 Venture Oral Dosing Study: Topline Result” portion from October 2025 Corporate Presentation the critical nuances that may bridge those two views: (i) the overwhelming majority (98%) of drug related TEAEs among VK2735 subjects are mild or moderate; (ii) treatment discontinuations are generally dose-dependent and may be related to titration rate; and (iii) GI AEs are generally observed early and subside over time (see the chart immediately below).

Let's dive further into the details:

  • Duration of the Trial - VKTX’s oral data were 13-week snapshots, not 26- or 72-week outcomes. GLP-1 efficacy is nonlinear; weight loss for the same molecule does not plateau at 13 weeks. An ~11% placebo-adjusted reduction at 13 weeks is very significant, and may well be the best in class in terms of efficacy.
  • Titration - With the benefit of hindsight, I can say with some modest confidence that the 15mg -120 mg cohort escalated too quickly. In the slower-ramp exploratory arm (30 → 60 → 90 → 30 mg), patients still posted ~9% mean loss at week 13 with far better retention.
  • Placebo Noise - The 18% placebo discontinuation is unusually high, which inflates the relative gap and hints at design and expectation effects. Some patients probably realized they were not losing weight and dropped out of the trial. As for placebo-arm gastrointestinal complaints, those likely reflect more psychological/expectation effects than any drug-related fundamental issues as VK2735 obviously cannot possibly be responsible for placebo’s purported adverse events.

As seen in the clinical-trial history of other weight-loss agents—especially oral GLP-1 agents—placebo attrition is not unusual in weight-management programs. Viking has already said it will slow titration and narrow down the dosage range from 12mg-120mg to 20mg-75mg. Such fine-tuning of the clinical design will probably substantially mitigate the ostensible discontinuance issues without compromising the efficacy data. In short, the discontinuation issue in oral VK2735 appears to have resulted from trial design, not a defect of the molecule.

Above and beyond the potential tolerability/discontinuation issues, we need to zoom out and note the big picture, as concisely summarized by the October 2025 Corporate Presentation,

  1. VK2735 Venture Oral Phase 2 Study has met both its primary and secondary endpoints;
  2. Up to 12.2% reduction in body weight was observed after merely 13 weeks of oral dosing (see chart immediately below from Page 14 of October 2025 Corporate Presentation);
  3. Sub-q to oral maintenance study is planned to begin in 3Q 2025.

The bottom line is that Viking does not need oral VK2735 to be “the best,” although it still holds that potential. It just needs to enter Phase 3, survive the titration learning curve, and ultimately be approved, whether as a standalone initial weight-loss agent or as the maintenance agent following its sub-q twin. Even as a maintenance agent after an initial injectable run, it can materially contribute to enlarging the bottom line of the company while shrinking the waistline of the country and the world.

Pipeline Overview

VK2735 — Injectable (sub-q)

This is the pillar of the Viking enterprise. In Phase 2, weekly sub-q VK2735 delivered up to ~14.7% mean weight loss at 13 weeks with a clean safety profile. Durability surprised to the upside: seven weeks after the last dose, patients maintained >90% of the loss, consistent with a long tissue half-life. That durability supports serious evaluation of once-monthly maintenance dosing after an initial weekly induction—a regimen patients and payors will welcome if it holds. The majority of GI-related adverse effects occur early in treatment and resolve themselves, which bodes well for oral Vk2735 in further clinical trials. (Source: Oct 2025 Corporate Presentation). Two Phase 3 studies are now underway, and the estimated primary completions will occur in 2027.

The company has cash in hand to finish both. It is noteworthy that those trials were rarely designed to evaluate transition to monthly or oral dosing regimens. If successful, either the monthly regimen or the oral delivery will offer a significant differentiator and competitive advantage to Viking.

My view is that sub-q VK2735 has a real shot at being best-in-class among second-wave entrants. If that sounds bold, it is because the efficacy is remarkable, and nothing about its oral twin’s (hopefully) temporary setback changes that.

VK2735 — Oral

As aforementioned, VENTURE-Oral proved the key point: the pill works. The discontinuation profile may be an issue, but it should be mitigated or even eliminated through better titration and dosing. For patients who start with sub-q VK2735 and transition to the oral form for maintenance (same molecule), tolerability should improve.

Will it match orforglipron’s eventual package? Maybe, maybe not. Does it need to? No. It needs to be good enough to be approved by the FDA. The reason is simple: maintenance is a huge market in and of itself. Viking can take a meaningful share of that market if sub-q 2735 is approved, and a substantial market share if sub-q can be administered monthly.

VK2809 — MASH

VK2809 (TR-β agonist) has already posted positive Phase 2b signals (liver fat, ALT, LDL). The regulatory context has improved: the FDA has opened the door to non-invasive surrogate endpoints such as liver stiffness, and Wegovy’s MASH approval formalizes the metabolic link.

With Roche paying $3.5 billion for 89bio recently, the strategic value in MASH is obvious. Viking prudently paused Phase 3 initiation while biopsy/endpoints are standardized. When properly aligned, VK2809 can become the second pillar of VKTX’s growth.

Capacity, Operations, and the “Go-It-Alone” Option

Many clinical-stage biotech companies falter while transitioning into commercial operations. Viking has mitigated that risk with a multi-year production agreement with CordenPharma that locks in industrial-scale supply for both oral and injectable VK2735: more than one billion oral tablets per year, 100 million autoinjectors per year, and 100 million vial/syringe units per year. Under the agreement, VKTX will make $150 million in prepayments from 2025 to 2028 to secure future orders.

That does not appear to be a perfunctory “press-release partnership.” It provides Viking with the manufacturing backbone upon the approval of VK2735 by the FDA, if received. It means Viking can play the long game independently if a partnership with, or acquisition by, Big Pharma does not materialize.

Valuation: Back-of-the-Envelope, But Not Hand-Wavy

At a $3 billion market cap, Viking is priced as if it is unlikely to succeed and barely surviving. I use a step-up valuation, I have stated before, because it is both transparent and conservative:

  • The GLP-1 category should reach around $100 billion globally by decade’s end.
  • A 5% share implies $5 billion in revenue.
  • Apply a 3× P/S multiple (a floor in buyouts) → $15 billion for VK2735 alone, before MASH or amylin.
  • VK2809 adds another shot on goal, with a cleaner regulatory runway than a year ago.

My deliberately conservative probability tree is as set forth below:

Program

Probability

Potential ($B)

Weighted ($B)

VK2735 Injectable

VK2735 Oral

"Rational" Current Market Cap

$10.17B

The following are my best estimates of different likely scenarios as to the future value of the company:

  • Base: sub-q wins; oral is acceptable (maintenance); MASH progresses → $12-15B fair value.
  • Upside: sub-q wins; oral is better than expected (adopted as a standalone initial agent for broader use, not just post-VK2735 sub-q maintenance—or, alternatively, as a maintenance agent for patients who achieved weight loss through non-VKTX agents); MASH positive → $25–$50B.
  • Downside: the company struggles as sub-q disappoints; oral is not established even for maintenance; MASH gains no serious traction; market cap sinks to well below $1B.

To the bears who argue that Lilly and Novo are miles ahead and that winner-take-all dynamics will prevail, I respectfully disagree. The essential question is not whether Viking outruns them, but whether there is room for a strong #3—even setting aside the dream scenario in which VK2735 knocks both Lilly and Novo from their leadership positions.

Ask Dr Pepper how that works in beverages: a durable third brand can still be immensely valuable if the category is large enough—and if you acquired the Dr Pepper of the weight-loss market at a fraction of its fair value before it left the gate. The markets for obesity drugs (and, coincidentally, sugar-loaded soda that has inflated the waistlines of our species) are both big and durable.

The Buyout Game: Big Pharma Or Private Equity

I speculate that Brian Lian, a scientist and former Wall Street insider, has probably driven a hard bargain with any potential suitors from the Big Pharma universe as he prepares the company to obtain FDA approvals and to market and deliver VK2735 on its own. The most powerful bargaining chip he has, after the clinical data of VK2735, is probably the deal with CordenPharma, as it represents the company's alternative to any inferior offer on the table.

I also wish to raise the possibility of a PE buyout. A $3 billion market value is within the reach of multi-fund PE complexes. Several funds together (or a single heavyweight) can pay more than Big Pharma, take Viking private, finish Phase 3 on both forms, and exit to public markets at a much higher implied value or keep the company as the cash cow.

Why would PE do it? The returns pencil if sub-q is approved—even without oral (which I still expect to survive). Viking is clean: cash, no debt, locked-in with industrial-scale production commitment. Dr. Brian Lian may prefer control and timing—PE can offer that in ways strategic buyers often do not.

The lack of any buyout may disappoint those who bought VKTX for a quick reward. However, if management, led by Dr. Lian, sees the entire clinical program and commercial launches through to fully realize the pipeline’s value, long-term investors will be handsomely rewarded.

The Cash Runway

At mid-2025, Viking reported approximately $808 million in cash and equivalents and a market capitalization near $2.8 billion. R&D expenses were rising into the $60 million-per-quarter range, consistent with launching two Phase III trials, while net operating cash outflow for the first half of 2025 was about $99 million. Even assuming a 30 percent increase in burn, Viking has a runway of four years or more — ample time to reach key clinical trial and regulatory approval milestones.

That war chest also creates optionality: Viking can partner selectively, negotiate from strength, or choose to go it alone through pivotal readouts.

Risks to My Thesis

At the risk of stating the obvious, investing in clinical-stage biotech companies is not for the faint of heart, or the impatient traders or smart chart readers (no offense), or for anyone who does not have the requisite patience to understand and analyze the clinical data. The following risks stand out:

  • Clinical Trials. Phase 3 clinical trials are difficult. Sub-q could miss a primary endpoint or reveal an unforeseen safety signal. Oral could remain “good but finicky” on tolerability despite titration discipline. VK2809’s further clinical trial design could slip as endpoints evolve.
  • Competitive. Novo’s oral semaglutide and Lilly’s orforglipron will continue to be formidable in the foreseeable future; newer tri-agonists are emerging. The crowding effect is real and can compress Viking’s addressable slice.
  • Operation and Execution. In addition to marketing and distribution, scaling to physically produce >1B tablets and/or ~100M devices annually is nothing trivial, even with a reputable contract manufacturer. The tech transfer, QA/QC, and other supply chain challenges all involve substantial execution risks for VKTX. Such risks have been somewhat mitigated, but not eliminated, by the CordenPharma deal structure.
  • Financing. While current cash funds the pivotal Phase III clinical trials, more financing will be required for commercial operations.
  • Psychology. If large drawdowns (see: –40% day) make you feel nauseated, you should not buy shares of VKTX—or, for that matter, even consider enrolling in a VK2735 clinical trial.

Conclusion

Viking stock appears to be a mispriced asymmetric bet with the odds favoring bulls. The probability that all three programs fail is not zero, but it is nowhere near Mr. Market’s implied odds. The aggregate of the probability-weighted value of the injectable VK2735, oral VK2735, and VK2809 far exceeds today’s market cap of VKTX.

Based upon the foregoing, I assign a speculative BUY to VKTX with a price target range between $100 and $200, depending on the clinical trial results, market sentiments, and other things. Any long-term investor of VKTX, however, does need the right temperament to be able to watch your position drop 40% of its value in a day, and still review the clinical data (and your brokerage statement) calmly without losing sight of both the big picture and the critical nuances.

On a final note, I plan to revisit this stock periodically as more clinical trial data become available.
